frameworks.name: Second-Level Thinking Framework
frameworks.description: Howard Marks' approach to finding non-consensus opportunities
process: 1. First-level thinking: What is the consensus view?
3. What would have to happen for consensus to be wrong?
4. What is the probability of the non-consensus scenario?
5. Is the non-consensus view priced into the stock?

example.first_level: It's a good company, let's buy the stock
example.second_level: It's a good company, but everyone thinks it's great. It's overrated and overpriced - let's sell

sources: Howard Marks - The Most Important Thing

frameworks.name: Sentiment Extreme Detection
frameworks.description: Identify when sentiment has reached actionable extremes

frameworks.name: Mean Reversion Framework
frameworks.description: Identify candidates for mean reversion trades
criteria: 3. Catalyst identified that could change narrative

frameworks.name: Value Trap vs Opportunity Assessment
frameworks.description: Distinguish cheap stocks from value traps
opportunity_signs: Cyclical downturn in otherwise healthy industry
Strong balance sheet to weather storm

frameworks.name: Market Cycle Positioning
frameworks.description: Templeton's framework for understanding where we are in the cycle

stages.pessimism: Bear markets end here. Time to buy aggressively.
stages.skepticism: Recovery begins but few believe it. Continue buying.
stages.optimism: Broad participation and rising prices. Hold positions.
stages.euphoria: Everyone bullish, excessive valuations. Time to sell.
